comment_text.sn10.4:1.2: Maṇibhadda means “auspicious jewel”. Yakkhas of this name are found in both Brahmanism and Jainism as bestowers of wealth. Hindu texts identify him as brother of Kuvera, king of yakkhas, and tutellary deity of merchants and travellers. Mahāvīra was once said to have stayed at a Maṇibhadra shrine, but that was in Mithila, north of the Ganges.
comment_text.sn10.4:2.1: Maṇibhadda plays on his own name, taking mindfulness to be auspicious, rather than jewels.
comment_text.sn10.4:4.1: The Buddha does not advocate mindfulness as the sole solution for all difficulties. Rather, specific problems require relevant antidotes, though of course mindfulness is always present.

root_text.sn10.4:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā magadhesu viharati maṇimālike cetiye maṇibhaddassa yakkhassa bhavane.
root_text.sn10.4:1.2: Atha kho maṇibhaddo yakkho yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:

translation_text.sn10.4:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Magadhans at the Maṇimālika Shrine, the haunt of the native spirit Maṇibhadda.
translation_text.sn10.4:1.2: Then the native spirit Maṇibhadda went up to the Buddha, and spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn10.4:2.3: Each new day is better for the mindful,
translation_text.sn10.4:2.4: and they are freed from enmity.”
translation_text.sn10.4:3.3: Each new day is better for the mindful,
translation_text.sn10.4:3.4: but they’re not freed from enmity.
translation_text.sn10.4:4.1: But someone whose mind delights in harmlessness,
translation_text.sn10.4:4.3: with love for all living creatures—
translation_text.sn10.4:4.4: they have no enmity for anyone.”
